english: to supernatural voices etc. Mil.; esp. to prophesy, predict, to reveal secrets, with termin.: dā-na-śī-la yín-par luṅ bstan (da na shi la yin par lung bstan) it is prophesied that it is Dānaśīla, the prediction relates to D., saṅs-rgyás-su luṅ-bstan-to (sangs rgyas su lung bstan to) he has received a prediction concerning (his obtaining) the Buddhaship Dzl.; mdaṅ mká-°groś luṅ-bstan-pai skyés-bu (mdang mkha' 'gros lung bstan pa'i skyes bu) the man foreshown yesterday by the Dākini Mil., hence luṅ-ma-bstan-pa (lung ma bstan pa) unheard of, unprecedented Mil. nt. (Cs. also: to demonstrate, luṅ-du brtán-du yód-pa (lung du brtan du yod pa) demonstrable?); luṅ gód-pa (lung 'god pa) Cs. to make, to establish, precepts; luṅ °drén-pa (lung 'dren pa) Cs. to cite, to quote, an authority Tar. 210, 2; luṅ-gi rjes-bráṅ (lung gi rjes brang) Was. (274) those who stick to the letter (opp. to rigs-kyi rjes-bráṅ (rigs kyi rjes brang) to the real quality, viz. the spirit); luṅ-bstán (lung bstan) exhortation, precept, commandment, lhai luṅ-bstán bśád-pa (lha'i lung bstan bshad pa) to communicate the precept of the god Tar., ... żes byá-bai luṅ-bstán byuṅ (zhes bya ba'i lung bstan byung) there came a divine order or prophecy of this purport, to this effect; hence luṅ-ston-pa (lung ston pa) prophet Chr. Prot.

1. valley, ri-luṅ (ri lung) mountain and valley; luṅ-čéṅ (lung chen) a large valley, luṅ-čúṅ (lung chung) or luṅ-prán (lung phran) a little valley; luṅ-kóg (lung kog) Sch.: 'the cavity of the valley'; luṅ-stóṅ (lung stong) a desolate, a solitary valley, as a fit abode for hermits, frq. -- 2. furrow, hollow, groove, e.g. on the surface of a stick Mil., of the liver Med.

manure, dung, lug-lúd (lug lud) sheep's dung; lud grém-pa (lud grem pa) to spread manure (on fields) Cs., lud °drén-pa (lud 'dren pa) to carry manure (to the fields) Cs., *gyáb-ċe (rgyab ce), táb-ċe (btab ce), táṅ-ċe* (btang ce) W. to manure the ground; lud-ku (lud ku) dung-water; lud-dóṅ (lud dong) dung-hole; lud-puṅ (lud phung) dung-hill; lúd-°bu (lud 'bu) grubs etc. in a dung-hill.

1. sbst. phlegm, mucus, rnag-krág-gi lúd-pas bkaṅ-ste (rnag khrag gi lud pas bkang ste) full of phlegm, matter and blood Glr.; esp. in the organs of respiration: lúd-pa ċíg bskyúr-bas (lud pa cig bskyur bas) throwing up some phlegm Glr.; lúd-pa lú-ba (lud pa lu ba) to throw up by coughing Dzl., sbríd-pa (sbrid pa) by sneezing S.g.; lúd-por (lud por) spittoon, spitting-box C. -- 2. vb., to boil over ču lúd-pas (chu lud pas) the water boiling over Dzl.; mtso lúd-pa (mtsho lud pa) the running over of lakes, inundation Ma.; ču lúd-nas lúd-nas bkán-ba yín (chu lud nas lud nas bkan ba yin) it filled, by the water rising higher and higher.
a bath used as a medical cure; fomentation.
n. of a queen, and of a grove called after her, situated in the north of India, where Buddha is said to have been born.

also lús-po (lus po), body, lus sá-la brdáb-pa (lus sa la brdab pa) to prostrate one's self, frq., lus stón-pa (lus ston pa) to show one's self, to appear, to make one's self visible, as gods Dzl., and in a similar manner lus (lus) is often used for expressing our reflective verbs, when relating to physical processes, cf. sems (sems); lús-kyi dbáṅ-po (lus kyi dbang po) the sense of feeling, in as far as it resides in the skin and the whole body of man Med.; rgyál-poi yduṅ-brgyúd (rgyal po'i gdung brgyud) (or rgyal-bu (rgyal bu)) lús-la yod (lus la yod) I bear a prince under my bosom Glr.; lus smád-pa (lus smad pa) to violate, to ravish Pth.; lus grúb-pai °tóg-ma (lus grub pa'i thog ma) the beginning of the development of a body as embryo Wdn.; grúb-pa lus (grub pa lus) v. grúb-pa (grub pa); lús-la ċágs-śiṅ (lus la chags shing) from love of life Dzl.; lus daṅ sróg-la sdó-ba (lus dang srog la sdo ba) to risk or stake one's life Dzl.; mí-lus tób-pa (mi lus thob pa) or bláṅ-ba (blang ba) to be born as a human being, lus-ṅán (lus ngan) (to be born) as an animal, or also as a woman Mil.; - often for the whole person of a man: brán-gyi lus kyaṅ dpón-du gyur (bran gyi lus kyang dpon du gyur) even a servant may become a master S.g.; lús-kyis mi bzód-par nya-ṅán-gyis ydúṅs-te (lus kyis mi bzod par mya ngan gyis gdungs te) is used (Dzl. 22, 2) of an exclusively mental suffering or infirmity. - In mysticism and speculative science several expressions are employed which, however, do not differ much in their import: sgyú-lus (sgyu lus), °já-lus ('ja' lus), bde-°gro mto-rís-kyi lus (bde 'gro mtho ris kyi lus); ríg-pa °dzin-pai lus (rig pa 'dzin pa'i lus) (Tar. 56, 20), yíd-kyi lus (yid kyi lus) (frq.), लिङ्गशरीर, the immaterial body which is enclosed in the grosser material frame, accompanying the soul in all its transmigrations and not destroyed by death (Köpp. I., 66), yid-kyi lus (yid kyi lus) might be rendered by 'spiritual body'; another explanation given by Lamas is: the <
